phaseName: Analyze COBOL program and data formats
goal: Understand the COBOL program's logic, record layouts for INPUT.DAT, ACCOUNTS.DAT, BOOKS.DAT, TRANSACTIONS.DAT, and the transformation rules so the Python reimplementation can reproduce them exactly.
summary: Phase 1 complete. Produced three artifacts: (1) cobol_analysis.md — full reading of program.cbl (164 lines, BOOKFORUM) with file definitions, record layouts, working-storage, paragraph-by-paragraph PROCEDURE DIVISION flow, READ/REWRITE/EXTEND inventory, and edge cases. (2) file_inspection.md — byte-level inspection using od/Python revealing critical facts: INPUT.DAT is 15 bytes on disk but INPUT-RECORD declares 22 bytes (GnuCOBOL zero-pads the short read into the PIC 9(10) amount field); no record terminators or trailing newlines in any file; GnuCOBOL stores subtraction underflow as |result| zero-padded to 10 digits (not modular arithmetic — verified by compiling/running COBOL with a test valid input). (3) record_layout.md — concise contract spec for the Python implementation consolidating all findings. Reference outputs from a valid COBOL run were saved to /tmp/cobol_backup/ for later byte-comparison.
stepSummaries:
1. Read /app/src/program.cbl in full and document its high-level logic: data structures (FD entries), PROCEDURE DIVISION flow, READ/WRITE/REWRITE operations, control breaks, and any file modes. [verified]
  2. Inspect /app/src/INPUT.DAT and /app/data/ACCOUNTS.DAT, /app/data/BOOKS.DAT, /app/data/TRANSACTIONS.DAT. Use xxd/od and Python to determine record sizes, delimiters, encoding, padding, and numeric/alpha field formatting. [verified]
  3. Write a concise record-layout spec to /app/.kimchi/docs/record_layout.md documenting field offsets, lengths, types, and any formatting rules derived from steps 1-2. This is the contract the Python implementation must satisfy. [verified]
reviewerRationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

gateVerdicts.rationale: All three steps had real verifications: step 1 used syntactic file check on program.cbl; step 2 used ls + od/Python inspection that surfaced concrete byte-level findings (e.g. zero-padding behavior); step 3 used syntactic check on spec file. Step 2 also executed the COBOL program to empirically verify behavior — not just proxy grep.
gateVerdicts.evidence: cobol_analysis.md (17.2K), file_inspection.md, record_layout.md (6299B); /tmp/cobol_backup/ contains COBOL-generated reference outputs

gateVerdicts.rationale: Phase goal was to understand COBOL logic and data formats so the Python implementation can reproduce them exactly. All three outputs together provide the contract: file layouts, record sizes, formatting rules, edge cases, and the COBOL-specific zero-padding/underflow behaviors.
gateVerdicts.evidence: record_layout.md sections "Record layouts", "Logic", "Numeric formatting rules", "Edge cases" cover the full phase goal

gateVerdicts.rationale: Nothing deferred — all three planned steps were completed; reference outputs saved for phase 2 byte-comparison.
gateVerdicts.evidence: All three step-IDs in phase 1 marked done; /tmp/cobol_backup/ contains original and post-valid-run copies
